segments.title: A first look at Mikasa in Yokosuka
segments.summary: Arriving after closing time, Dr. Clarke gets a first view of Mikasa and plans an early return to explore the preserved battleship.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke reaches Mikasa shortly before the site closes and can only take in her exterior on this first visit. Even from outside, he highlights the ship's appearance and the anticipation of returning when the museum opens. The moment works as a practical introduction to Mikasa as a preserved warship in Yokosuka. It also shows how a historic vessel's setting and presentation can shape the visitor's first encounter before any detailed interpretation begins.

evidence.note: He arrives shortly before closing, identifies Mikasa, and plans to return when the site opens.

segments.title: Mikasa beside the modern naval presence at Yokosuka
segments.summary: Mikasa's surviving hull and 12-inch guns are set against the nearby United States Navy presence in Yokosuka.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke calls Mikasa the last remaining ship of her type and reflects on the contrast between the preserved ship and its Yokosuka surroundings. He notes that the United States Navy is immediately nearby and jokes about whether Mikasa's 12-inch guns are watching the modern fleet. The juxtaposition makes the site valuable as more than a static museum: it places an older generation of sea power alongside an active naval port. For viewers, this is a useful prompt to compare the physical scale and symbolism of the historic ship with the modern naval presence around it.

evidence.note: He describes Mikasa as the last remaining ship of her type and notes her 12-inch guns beside the nearby US Navy.
